title: Introduction to the Book of Revelation
subtitle: A concise guide to Revelation's name, author, date, occasion, structure, and central burden.
summary: Revelation is the unveiling of Jesus Christ, given through John to seven real churches and to the whole church that hears what the Spirit says. It was written from Patmos near the close of the first century, when believers faced pressure from empire, false worship, compromise, and suffering. The book opens with Christ among His churches, moves through the sanctuary-shaped conflict of history, and ends with His visible triumph and the New Jerusalem.

facts.label: Name
facts.value: Revelation means unveiling; the book reveals Jesus Christ and His work in history.

facts.label: Author
facts.value: John, the apostolic witness and exiled prophet on Patmos.

facts.label: Date
facts.value: Late first century, around A.D. 95-96, under Domitian-era pressure.

facts.label: Occasion
facts.value: Churches facing worship pressure, compromise, persecution, and the need for endurance.

facts.label: Audience
facts.value: The seven churches of Asia and all who hear what the Spirit says to the churches.

facts.label: Theme
facts.value: Jesus reveals, judges, preserves, conquers, and restores; the central crisis is worship and allegiance.

highlights: Revelation begins with Christ before it speaks of beasts, plagues, Babylon, or the city.
The book addresses real first-century churches and the continuing church under Christ's searching care.
Daniel, the sanctuary, prophetic time, and the Old Testament prophets supply Revelation's symbolic grammar.
The final appeal is worship: follow the Lamb, keep God's commandments, and hope in Christ's return.

sections.title: Purpose
body: The opening words identify the book as the revelation of Jesus Christ. Revelation means unveiling. The book is not presented as a sealed puzzle for speculation, but as a disclosure God intended His servants to receive, hear, and keep. It reveals Christ's person, Christ's ministry, Christ's authority over history, and Christ's final victory.

The title also guards the way the book should be read. Revelation contains beasts, horns, trumpets, plagues, Babylon, a mark, a millennium, and a holy city, but none of these symbols should be allowed to displace Jesus from the center. He is the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, the priestly Son of Man among the lampstands, the slain Lamb, the heavenly minister, the conquering King, and the light of the New Jerusalem.

Its purpose is pastoral and prophetic at once. It blesses those who read, hear, and keep its words. It calls churches to repentance, warns against false worship, reveals the conflict behind history, announces judgment, and gives hope to the faithful. The book is meant to form worship, courage, discernment, obedience, mission, and endurance.

sections.title: Author and Date
body: The writer identifies himself as John, a servant of God and brother to the churches in tribulation, kingdom, and patient endurance. The strongest reading understands him as John the apostolic witness, now functioning as a prophet who bears testimony to what he has seen. He writes not to elevate himself, but to pass on the revelation entrusted to him.

John writes from Patmos, an island associated with exile, because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ. That setting matters. Revelation is not written from comfort or political power. It comes from a faithful witness under pressure, speaking to churches that also needed courage under pressure.

The book fits best near the close of the first century, around A.D. 95-96. The condition of the churches, the developed pressure of emperor-centered loyalty, and the ancient testimony that places John's exile late in the first century all support this setting. Revelation therefore begins in a real historical crisis while looking far beyond that crisis to the end of the age.

sections.title: Occasion
body: The immediate audience is the seven churches in Asia: Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamos, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea. These were real congregations in real cities. They lived amid trade, civic religion, social expectation, imperial honor, local persecution, false teaching, wealth, poverty, and spiritual fatigue.

The number seven also signals completeness. Each church receives a particular message, yet every message ends with the command to hear what the Spirit says to the churches. That repeated appeal widens the audience. Revelation is for the local churches of John's day and for the whole church that must listen to Christ's searching word across time.

The occasion is therefore both immediate and larger. Some believers needed comfort under suffering. Some needed repentance from compromise. Some needed warning against false teaching. Some needed to wake from spiritual complacency. All needed to see that Christ walks among His lampstands, governs history from heaven, and will bring the conflict to a righteous end.

sections.title: Shape
body: Revelation 1 introduces the book, gives the blessing, greets the churches, and shows the risen Christ among the lampstands. Revelation 2-3 records Christ's messages to the seven churches. These chapters establish the pastoral setting for everything that follows.

Revelation 4-5 opens the heavenly throne room and shows the Lamb worthy to take the scroll. Revelation 6-7 opens the seals and answers the question of who can stand. Revelation 8-11 introduces the trumpets, the altar scene, the little book, the two witnesses, and the seventh trumpet's announcement of Christ's kingdom.

Revelation 12-14 forms the center of the book's conflict: the woman, the dragon, the remnant, the sea beast, the earth beast, the mark, the Lamb on Mount Zion, the three angels' messages, and the harvest. Revelation 15-18 presents the seven last plagues and the fall of Babylon. Revelation 19-22 shows Christ's visible return, the defeat of rebellion, the millennium, the final judgment, the New Jerusalem, and the closing invitation of grace.

sections.title: Themes
body: The first major theme is the sovereignty of God and the victory of the Lamb. Earthly powers appear fierce, but they never outrank the throne. Christ conquers through sacrifice, truth, intercession, judgment, and righteous authority. His victory defines the destiny of His people.

A second major theme is worship and allegiance. Revelation contrasts the worship of the Creator with the worship of the beast, the seal of God with the mark of the beast, the Father's name with the beast's name, and the New Jerusalem with Babylon. The final conflict is not merely political; it is a test of loyalty, worship, law, and witness.

A third major theme is the sanctuary-shaped movement of the book. Lampstands, altar, incense, ark, temple, throne, intercession, judgment, and glory appear at key moments. Revelation's warnings and judgments are not random disasters. They unfold under Christ's heavenly ministry and God's covenant faithfulness.

sections.title: Reading Revelation
body: Revelation should be read with the rest of Scripture open. Daniel supplies the kingdom sequence, beast imagery, heavenly court, sanctuary concern, prophetic time, and final deliverance that Revelation develops. Exodus supplies plague and deliverance patterns. The prophets supply Babylon, restoration, judgment, and new-creation language.

The symbols should be read carefully, not sensationally. Revelation uses symbolic language to reveal real truth. It does not invite private invention or headline-driven speculation. The safer path is to follow the biblical echoes, observe the structure of the visions, and let Christ's own emphasis on worship, witness, endurance, and obedience guide the reading.

The book should also be read devotionally. Its goal is not merely to increase information. It calls the reader to worship God, trust the Lamb, resist counterfeit worship, endure under pressure, keep the words of the prophecy, and answer Christ's promise with hope: Even so, come, Lord Jesus.
